any review must assess whether the framing and gap statement are coherent and accurately scoped.","R1.2":"The logical chain from unsupervised preference signal → router training → benchmark generalisation must be internally consistent; the move from 'disagreement as difficulty proxy' to 'heavy-detector quality' is a non-trivial inferential step that warrants scrutiny.","R1.3":"Specific quantitative claims (53.4 mAP at 38% FLOPs; 71 ms vs 184 ms; within 0.4 mAP on LVIS at 30% cost) must be checked for alignment with the stated experimental setup; the absence of error bars or significance indicators is a notable gap.","R1.4":"The 'first' novelty claim is strong and falsifiable; the related-work paragraph names Viola-Jones, BranchyNet, SkipNet, MSDNet, and dynamic routing but does not cite concurrent image-level routing work (e.g., GFNet, LiteEval), so coverage and delta-framing need evaluation.","R1.6":"The section is technically dense and shifts register between related-work survey, method sketch, and result reporting without strong signposting; sentence- and paragraph-level clarity is material to whether a reviewer or reader can follow the argument.","R1.7":"Citation coverage directly underpins the novelty claim; the related-work pass is selective and several omissions (e.g., learned anytime networks, cascaded RCNN as a routing baseline) may undercut the 'first' assertion."},"clarification_needed":true,"clarification_question":"Is this primarily a technical review — checking logical consistency, evidence-claim alignment, and novelty validation — rather than an editorial or writing-quality review?"}

rationale.R1.1: The closing contribution paragraph makes a strong thesis claim ('first preference-trained image-level router … obviates ground-truth supervision'); any review must assess whether the framing and gap statement are coherent and accurately scoped.
rationale.R1.2: The logical chain from unsupervised preference signal → router training → benchmark generalisation must be internally consistent; the move from 'disagreement as difficulty proxy' to 'heavy-detector quality' is a non-trivial inferential step that warrants scrutiny.
rationale.R1.3: Specific quantitative claims (53.4 mAP at 38% FLOPs; 71 ms vs 184 ms; within 0.4 mAP on LVIS at 30% cost) must be checked for alignment with the stated experimental setup; the absence of error bars or significance indicators is a notable gap.
rationale.R1.4: The 'first' novelty claim is strong and falsifiable; the related-work paragraph names Viola-Jones, BranchyNet, SkipNet, MSDNet, and dynamic routing but does not cite concurrent image-level routing work (e.g., GFNet, LiteEval), so coverage and delta-framing need evaluation.
rationale.R1.6: The section is technically dense and shifts register between related-work survey, method sketch, and result reporting without strong signposting; sentence- and paragraph-level clarity is material to whether a reviewer or reader can follow the argument.
rationale.R1.7: Citation coverage directly underpins the novelty claim; the related-work pass is selective and several omissions (e.g., learned anytime networks, cascaded RCNN as a routing baseline) may undercut the 'first' assertion.

parsed.clarification_question: Is this primarily a technical review — checking logical consistency, evidence-claim alignment, and novelty validation — rather than an editorial or writing-quality review?
